title: Proposal: 2x Acceleration for FST Waveform Dumping (Native C++ Rewrite)
body:
Hi maintainers (@wsnyder),

My friend @yodalee and I have been profiling the FST waveform dumping performance in Verilator.

**The Problem:** Verilator simulates logic using native C++ integer types (CData, IData, QData, etc.), but the current verilated_fst_c implementation (derived from gtkwave's C API) relies heavily on string-based data structures for intermediate storage. This creates a significant bottleneck: every signal update forces an expensive "Integer-to-String" conversion just to pass data to the writer, even before the actual FST compression happens.

**The Proposal:** We have implemented a native C++ rewrite of the FST writer. Key Architectural Change: Instead of using strings as the intermediate format, our implementation uses strongly-typed uintX_t native data structures (e.g., uint8_t, uint32_t, uint64_t). This aligns the writer's internal storage directly with Verilator’s simulation kernel, eliminating the serialization overhead entirely. You can view our proof-of-concept snapshot here: https://github.com/yodalee/verilator_snapshot

**Current Results:** In our local benchmarks using a pico-riscv core simulation, this architecture change achieves 1.5-2x speedup in dumping time compared to the current string-based implementation, even when I have not implemented compression yet.

**Validation Strategy:** To ensure the output FST is valid:

1. We convert both "legacy" (string-based) and "new" (uint-based) FST outputs to VCD using fst2vcd.
2. We perform a diff on the resulting VCD files. So far, the waveform logic remains identical. 

**Licensing:** The new implementation is a derivative of the existing logic already present in the Verilator source tree. I intend to contribute this under the same license as Verilator (LGPL/Artistic).

**Status & Questions:** The code is functional but still in a "Work in Progress" state.

1. **Integration:** Given the fundamental change in data structure (String vs. uintX_t), do you prefer we introduce this as an optional backend first?
2. **Compatibility:** Are there specific test cases in the regression suite that heavily rely on the string-based behavior (e.g., very specific formatting of X/Z states) that we should be aware of?

Looking forward to your feedback!

Best regards, Yu-Sheng Lin
